pdf_content:
Release Notes for V7 of  

the Goddard Convective-Stratiform Heating (CSH) Algorithm  

(2HCSH, 3GCSH, 3HCSH)  

1 May 2022 

1. 

Tropical/warm season retrievals 

For V7, the Goddard CSH algorithm is largely unchanged from V6 with respect to tropical/warm season 
retrievals in terms of algorithm structure and metrics with the exception of radiation retrievals, which were 
significantly revised (see below).  Thus, the V7 algorithm continues to follow the basic Tao et al. (2010) 
methodology of separating cloud heating/moistening profiles (latent and eddy components) into convective 
and stratiform, which are then separated with respect to 36 surface rainfall intensity bins (20 mm/day each).  
These look-up-tables (LUTs) of cloud heating profiles are composited into land and ocean categories.  As 
with V6, all retrievals are performed starting at the pixel level, so columns are either completely convective 
or stratiform. The convective-stratiform separation continues to follow V6, which employed a 2A23-like 
methodology  more  consistent  with  the  satellite  (PR/DPR)  separation  versus  the  Goddard  Cumulus 
Ensemble  (GCE)  model  separation  method  used  previously.    The  2A23-like  separation  first  applies 
Steiner’s method (Steiner et al. 1995) to composite reflectivities below the freezing level (h-type method).  
Second, a simple bright band approximation based on the vertical reflectivity gradient near the melting 
level (v-type method) is used to differentiate pixels (model subdomains) adjacent to convective cores.  If 
there is no BBand, pixels remain convective; if there is a BBand, pixels become stratiform; cores always 
remain convective.  The h-type method is primary; the v-method is only used to adjust regions immediately 
adjacent to convective cores.  Also following V6, the land and ocean LUTs are further binned according to 
mean  echo  top  heights  (5  bins,  0-2,  2-4,  4-6,  6-8,  and  8+  km)  and  low-level  (0-2  km  AGL)  vertical 
reflectivity gradients (downward increasing or decreasing) to improve the depth of heating and intensity of 
low-level evaporative cooling in stratiform regions (Lang and Tao 2018), respectively.  The vertical dBZ 
gradient is also  usual for  differentiating  younger  (more  intense  heating)  from  older convection  (weaker 
heating). 

For V7, the CSH algorithm LUTs are extracted from a new quasi-3D database.  This database encompasses 
the same 6 ocean and 4 land multi-week cases as V6 (Table 1), but using a horizontal GCE model domain 
of  640  km  (x-direction)  x  80  km  (y-direction)  at  1.25  km  resolution  (200  m  was  used  for  the  2D  V6 
database).  The stretched vertical grid using 70 levels remains the same.  The same  improved Goddard 
4ICE (cloud ice, snow,  graupel and hail) scheme (Lang et al. 2014; Tao et al. 2016) cloud microphysics 
was used in V7.  For V7, the GCE horizontal domain was divided into 5 km x 5 km subdomains when 
extracting the LUT profiles to better match the PR/DPR surface footprint size; V6 used subdomains of 4 
km (2D).  Table 2 outlines the similarities and differences between the CSH  V6 and V7 tropical/warm 
season retrievals. 

1 

 
 
 
 Table 1     GCE model simulated cases used to build the CSH tropical/warm season look-up tables.  Adapted 
from Lang and Tao (2018). 

Table 2    Parameter listing for the CSH algorithm V6 and V7 tropical/warm season retrievals.  Key changes 
for V7 are highlighted in RED. 

2.  High latitude/cold season retrievals 

Like the tropical/warm season retrievals, the algorithm for the V7 high latitude/cold season retrievals is 
fundmentally the same as V6 (Tao et al. 2019) with the exception of the new radiation retrievals.  However, 
in addition for V7, eddy heating was also included in the cold season retrievals, which was not included in 
V6.  The cold season LUTs are constructed and mapped using the following metrics:  echo top heights (11 
bins, 0-1, 1-2,…,10+ km), freezing level height (13 bins, at sfc, 0-500, 500-1000, …, 5500+ m), level of 
max dBZ (8 bins), vertical dBZ gradient (2 bins, downward decreasing by at least 10 dBZ from max value 

2 

 
 
 
 
 or NOT), surface precipitation rate (17 bins) and composite dBZ for intensity (35 bins, every 2 dBZ from 
10 to 78 dBZ).    

As  with  V6,  V7  cold  season  LUTs  are  extracted  from  a  set  of  6  NU-WRF  (NASA-Unified  Weather 
Research  and  Forecasting  Model)  simulations  (3  eastern  US  synoptic  snow  storms  and  3  West  Coast 
atmospheric river events) using the same improved 4ICE scheme in the warm season GCE database.  The 
NU-WRF simulations have nested grids, and the cold season LUTs are built from the inner-most 3 km grid.  
Following V6, the transition between warm and cold season retrievals in V7 is based on the height of the 
freezing level (FL).  FL > 4 km (=warm), FL < 3 km (=cold) with a linear weighting applied when the FL 
is between 3 and 4 km.  Table 3 lists the cases used for the cold season database; Table 4 outlines the 
similarities and differences between the CSH V6 and V7 extratropical/cold season retrievals. 

Table 3    List of six NU-WRF simulations constituting the V7 CSH high latitude/cold season database.   

Table 4    Parameter listing for the CSH algorithm V6 and V7 extratropical/cold season retrievals.  Key 
changes for V7 are highlighted in RED. 

3 

 
 
  
 3. 

New radiation 

For V7, the radiation retrievals have been significantly upgraded.  First, radiation is now separated into its 
long-wave  (LW)  and  short-wave  (SW)  solar  components  versus  being  composited  together  in  V6.  
Furthermore, the SW retrievals at the instantaneous pixel scale are based on the local solar zenith angle; 
they were not in V6.  Second, the new V7 radiation retrievals are built using metrics of echo tops and echo 
bottoms only with bins of 0-1,1-2,…, 10-11, 11+ km, for both.  The SW LUTs also incorporate 10 bins for 
the cosine of the local solar zenith angle (every 0.1).  For historical reasons, radiation depended on surface 
rainfall  in  V6  and  earlier  as  it  was  treated  as  a  component  of    Q1,  which  was  retrieved  using  rainfall 
normalized profiles (still true for the LH and eddy terms).  There are three separate sets of radiation LUTs:  
warm  season  ocean,  warm  season  land,  and  cold  season,  which  are  built  identically  from  the  different 
databases.  Figure 1 shows sample SW and LW LUTs for warm season ocean. 

Figure 1    Sample warm season oceanic LUTs for the new V7 detailed radiation retrievals.  Top row shows 
profiles  of  solar  heating  rates  as  a  function  of  local  solar  zenith  angle  (x-axis)  and  height  (y-axis)  for 
various echo top/echo bottom pairings.  Bottom row shows profiles of longwave cooling/heating rates as a 
function of echo bottom (x-axis) and height (y-axis) for various echo top heights. 

4. 

Terrain 

To make it easier on users, eliminate confusion and prepare for future retrievals over topography, V7 cloud 
heating  profiles  are  now  “terrain  aware”.    This  means  valid  V7  CSH-retrieved  cloud  heating/cooling 
profiles  are  stacked  on  top  of  the  local  terrain  relative  to  the  earth  ellipsoid  with  missing  values  now 

4 

 
 
 
 
 appearing at lower levels in columns having terrain.  Previously, the CSH retrievals were treated as being 
“above ground level” based on the fact that the GCE model does not have terrain.  Also, previously it was 
simply  stated  in  the  caveats  that  CSH  retrievals  in  high  terrain  were  not  to  be  relied  upon.    To  reduce 
ambiguity, all columns having terrain in excess of 1000 m are now set to missing in V7.  Note that the cold 
season LUTs are derived only from areas in the NU-WRF simulations were the terrain was < 500 m.  

5.  Heat balance 

Direct validation of cloud heating profiles is extremely challenging.  However, the heat budget equation 
from Yanai et al. (1973) can be used to ascertain overall biases in the retrieved cloud heating profiles.  This 
is done by converting column-integrated cloud heating into equivalent surface precipitation rates.  Figure 2 
shows  zonal-average  vertically-integrated  cloud  heating  versus  input  surface  precipitation  from  the 
Combined Radar-Radiometer Algorithm (CORRA) for both V6 and V7.  There are slight biases between 
the CSH integrated cloud heating and surface precipitation; however, the overall heat balance is good for 
both  V6  and  V7,  indicating  that  the  total  amount  of  retrieved  cloud  heating  is  approximately  correct.  
However, this necessary condition is not equivalent to direct validation as many different combinations of 
heating profile shapes and horizontal distribution can result in the same integrated amount. 

Figure 2    Total (i.e., land + ocean) zonal average column integrated CSH Q1-Qr (i.e., LH + eddy heating) 
versus Combined Algorithm surface precipitation for both V6 and V7. 

5 

 
 
 
 
 
 6. 

CSH Products 

For V7, the same 3 standard V6 CSH GPM products will be produced:  2HCSH (instantaneous, pixel), 
3GCSH (gridded), and 3HCSH (monthly, gridded).  There will also continue to be a corresponding set of 
TRMM products (based on LUTs built with slightly different minimum detection thresholds for echoes and 
surface  precipitation).    CSH  products  are  officially  produced  using  precipitation  retrievals  from  the 
Combined  Radar-Radiometer  Algorithm  (CORRA).    The  CSH  algorithm  relies  on  radar  data  from  the 
PR/DPR and reanalysis data that is passed through CORRA to obtain information on echo top heights, 
vertical  reflectivity  gradients  and  freezing  level  height  in  addition  to  the  precipitation  estimates  from 
CORRA.  Table 5 lists the CSH product specifics. 

Product

Input

 Eddy Heating (H+V)

 Micro Moistening* 
Eddy Moistening (H+V)* 
Same as above 

3GCSH

80 vertical levels 

3HCSH 

0.25 x 0.25 degrees 
80 vertical levels

CORRA

CORRA 

Same as above 

Table 5   Listing of CSH V7 products and retrieved fields.  *only retrieved within the TRMM domain (i.e., 

between 37o N and 37o S. 

Caveats: 

As CSH retrievals are derived from Level 2 products from the Combined Radar-Radiometer Algorithm, 
users are encouraged to check the latest CORRA documentation. 

Despite the screening out of CSH retrievals over higher terrain (i.e., terrain > 1000 m) in V7, retrievals in 
areas of steep terrain should be used with caution. 

Moistening rates are not retrieved poleward of 37o N/S, and surface fluxes are not included in the retrievals 
though they are part the simulations in the model databases. 

References:  

Lang, S., W.-K. Tao, J.-D. Chern, D. Wu, and X. Li, 2014:  Benefits of a 4th ice class in the simulated 
radar reflectivities of convective systems using a bulk microphysics scheme.  J. Atmos. Sci., 71, 
3583-3612.  doi: http://dx.doi.org/10.1175/JAS-D-13-0330.1

Tao, W.-K., T. Iguchi, and S. Lang, 2019: Expanding the Goddard CSH Algorithm for GPM: New

0215.
